reference_answer: Jabez Wilson.
supporting_source_spans: opening scene: Watson sees Holmes with a red-haired visitor; Wilson testimony: Holmes addresses him as Mr. Jabez Wilson

reference_answer: Four pounds a week, stated in the advertisement as a salary of £4 a week.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: Red-Headed League advertisement offering salary of £4 a week

reference_answer: Saxe-Coburg Square, also referred to as Coburg Square in the bank branch name context.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: pawnbroker business in Saxe-Coburg Square; Saxe-Coburg visit: Holmes and Watson visit Wilson's shop

reference_answer: He was required to copy out the Encyclopædia Britannica, beginning with the letter A.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: Duncan Ross assigns Wilson to copy the Encyclopædia Britannica

reference_answer: The City and Suburban Bank, specifically the branch/cellar adjoining Saxe-Coburg Square.
supporting_source_spans: Saxe-Coburg visit: Holmes observes the City and Suburban Bank; cellar climax: Merryweather identifies the bank cellar

reference_answer: April 27, 1890, in The Morning Chronicle.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: Holmes asks Watson to note the paper and date; Wilson says The Morning Chronicle of April 27, 1890

reference_answer: Eight weeks. Wilson says, "Eight weeks passed away like this."
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: work routine and eight-week duration

reference_answer: October 9, 1890.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: notice reading THE RED-HEADED LEAGUE IS DISSOLVED. October 9, 1890

reference_answer: Saturday night. Holmes says Saturday would suit them best and expects them to come that night.
supporting_source_spans: Baker Street epilogue: Holmes explains why Saturday suited the criminals

reference_answer: John Clay. Holmes seizes the intruder and says, "It is no use, John Clay."
supporting_source_spans: cellar climax: Holmes catches the first intruder emerging through the floor

reference_answer: Peter Jones, the official police agent from Scotland Yard.
supporting_source_spans: Baker Street preparation: Watson recognizes Peter Jones, the official police agent

reference_answer: Mr. Merryweather, chairman of the bank directors.
supporting_source_spans: Baker Street preparation: Holmes introduces Mr. Merryweather; cellar setup: Merryweather describes the bank's gold

reference_answer: Wilson was hired to lure him out of his pawnshop for several hours each day so Clay and his accomplice could dig a tunnel from the shop cellar to the bank.
supporting_source_spans: Baker Street epilogue: Holmes explains the advertisement, copying work, and Wilson's daily absence as the criminals' object

reference_answer: The League dissolved because the tunnel was complete and the criminals no longer needed Wilson kept away from the shop.
supporting_source_spans: Baker Street epilogue: Holmes says closing the League offices showed they had completed their tunnel

reference_answer: Holmes expected Saturday because the criminals needed to use the tunnel soon, and Saturday would give them two days for escape before the robbery was discovered.
supporting_source_spans: Baker Street epilogue: Holmes explains the Saturday timing and two-day escape window

reference_answer: He would forfeit the position forever if he left the office during working hours.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: Duncan Ross states that absence from the office means forfeiture forever

reference_answer: Wilson's bachelor status nearly disqualified him; Ross says it is unfortunate but makes an exception because of Wilson's red hair.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: Ross comments that Wilson is a bachelor and then waives the problem in favour of his head of hair

reference_answer: 30,000 napoleons from the Bank of France. The text does not state a pound-sterling equivalent for that total.
supporting_source_spans: cellar setup: Merryweather says the bank borrowed 30,000 napoleons from the Bank of France

reference_answer: Wilson got through entries around the A section: Abbots, Archery, Armour, Architecture, and Attica, hoping to reach the B's.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: Wilson describes the eight weeks of copying and the entries reached

reference_answer: Duncan Ross and William Morris. Ross is the name used at the League office; the landlord later says the red-headed man's name was William Morris.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: advertisement names Duncan Ross; Wilson follow-up: landlord identifies the same office occupant as William Morris

reference_answer: Vincent Spaulding is John Clay, the criminal Holmes captures in the bank cellar.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: assistant named Vincent Spaulding; Baker Street preparation: Jones describes John Clay; cellar climax: Holmes names the captured intruder John Clay; Baker Street epilogue: Holmes links the assistant to Clay

reference_answer: No. Within the story, Ezekiah Hopkins and the bequest are part of the fabricated Red-Headed League cover; there is no independent real benefactor established.
supporting_source_spans: Wilson testimony: advertisement claims a bequest by Ezekiah Hopkins; Baker Street epilogue: Holmes calls the League business a fantastic advertisement and copying scheme used as a blind

reference_answer: Watson frames the case as having occurred "one day in the autumn of last year," so the narration is retrospective relative to the events; the exact narration date is not specified in the story text.
supporting_source_spans: opening scene: Watson says he called on Holmes one day in the autumn of last year

reference_answer: Watson is a first-person observer. He reports what he sees, what Wilson tells Holmes, and what Holmes later explains; Holmes withholds much of his reasoning until after the arrest.
supporting_source_spans: opening scene: Watson narrates his visit; Saxe-Coburg visit: Watson observes without knowing Holmes's purpose; Baker Street epilogue: Holmes explains the reasoning after the event

reference_answer: The reader learns the tunnelling plan through Holmes's post-arrest explanation to Watson, not through direct narration of the criminals planning it.
supporting_source_spans: Baker Street epilogue: Holmes reconstructs Clay's motive, cellar work, tunnel, and timing after the cellar arrest
